scanner_description: Detects patterns AI tools generate that ship to production — SQL concatenation, missing awaits on auth checks, weak fallbacks, commented-out security, debug modes, default Docker creds, cryptography misuse (Math.random for tokens, MD5/SHA1 for passwords, ECB mode, hardcoded IVs).

rules.name: jwt.decode() Used Instead of jwt.verify()
rules.note: jwt.decode() does not validate the signature. Anyone can forge a valid-looking token. AI tools confuse decode() and verify() constantly.
rules.remediation: Replace jwt.decode(token) with jwt.verify(token, secret, { algorithms: ['HS256'] })

rules.name: SQL Query Built from String Concatenation
rules.note: User input concatenated into SQL query — the #1 AI-generated vulnerability. Looks functional, fully exploitable via SQL injection.
rules.remediation: Use parameterized queries: db.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = $1', [userId])

rules.name: Env Variable with Weak Fallback Default
rules.note: process.env.X || 'secret' — if env var missing in prod, app silently uses the weak default. AI adds this for dev convenience.
rules.remediation: Remove the fallback. Crash on startup if required env vars are missing: if (!process.env.JWT_SECRET) throw new Error('JWT_SECRET required')

rules.name: bcrypt Rounds Set Too Low
rules.note: bcrypt cost factor under 10 — brute-forceable in seconds on modern hardware. Catches both bcrypt(8) and bcrypt.hash(password, 8) forms. AI sets low rounds for 'speed during development.'
rules.remediation: Use minimum 12 rounds: bcrypt.genSalt(12) / bcrypt.hash(pw, 12). Recommended: 12-14 for 2024+ hardware.

rules.name: Session Secret Set to Placeholder Value
rules.note: Session secret is a well-known placeholder. AI generates these — they ship to production and allow session forging.
rules.remediation: Generate: require('crypto').randomBytes(64).toString('hex'). Store in env var.

rules.name: TODO: Auth/Security Not Implemented
rules.note: Comment indicates security feature was planned but never implemented. Common AI pattern: generate a TODO, move on, ship.
rules.remediation: Implement the security feature before deploying. Don't ship TODOs.

rules.name: Auth Middleware Commented Out
rules.note: Authentication middleware is present but commented out. Likely disabled during testing and never re-enabled.
rules.remediation: Uncomment the auth middleware. Use environment-based bypasses instead of commenting out.

rules.name: console.log of Sensitive Data
rules.note: Debug logging of passwords, tokens, or secrets left in code. Extremely common AI-generated debug pattern.
rules.remediation: Remove console.log of sensitive data. Use structured logging with redaction in production.

rules.name: Async Auth Check Without Await
rules.note: Auth check called but not awaited — returns Promise<true> which is truthy, so auth always passes. Silent bypass.
rules.remediation: Add await: const isAuth = await checkAuth(req). Ensure the check blocks the handler.

rules.name: CORS Set to Reflect Origin or Wildcard
rules.note: CORS dynamically reflects the requesting origin or uses wildcard — nullifies all CORS protection. AI does this to 'fix' CORS errors.
rules.remediation: Set explicit allowed origins: cors({ origin: ['https://yourdomain.com'] })

rules.name: Missing await on Security Check in Condition
rules.note: Async security check used in if() without await. Promise object is truthy — auth always passes regardless of actual result.
rules.remediation: Add await: if (await isAuthenticated(req)) { ... }

rules.name: Debug Mode in Production Config (NODE_ENV=development)
rules.note: NODE_ENV set to development in a config file that may be deployed to production. Exposes stack traces, internal paths, and debug endpoints.
rules.remediation: Set NODE_ENV=production in production configs.

rules.name: Debug Mode in Production Config (FLASK_DEBUG=1)
rules.note: Flask debug enabled in a config file that may be deployed to production. Exposes the Werkzeug debugger, which allows arbitrary code execution.
rules.remediation: Set FLASK_DEBUG=0 in production configs.

rules.name: Debug Mode in Production Config (DEBUG=True)
rules.note: Python DEBUG=True in a config file that may be deployed to production. Common Django/Flask pitfall.
rules.remediation: Set DEBUG=False in production configs.

rules.name: Debug Mode in Production Config (APP_DEBUG=true)
rules.note: Laravel APP_DEBUG=true in a config file that may be deployed to production. Exposes stack traces, env vars, and DB queries.
rules.remediation: Set APP_DEBUG=false in production configs.

rules.name: Default Database Credentials in Docker Config
rules.note: Default/weak database password in docker-compose.yml or Dockerfile. If this file is committed, anyone can access the database.
rules.remediation: Use a strong random password. Reference env vars: ${DB_PASSWORD} with .env file.

rules.name: Firebase Open Read/Write Rules
rules.note: Firebase rules allow read/write to anyone. Every person on the internet can read and write your entire database.
rules.remediation: Replace with ownership-based rules: allow read, write: if request.auth != null && request.auth.uid == resource.data.userId;

rules.name: Math.random() Used for Token / Secret / ID Generation
rules.note: Math.random() is a Mersenne Twister — predictable from any handful of outputs. Tokens, password reset codes, invite IDs, and session IDs generated this way are guessable. AI writes 'Math.random().toString(36).substring(2)' constantly because it 'looks random'.
rules.remediation: Use crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex') for tokens, or crypto.randomUUID() for IDs. Both are CSPRNG-backed and unpredictable.

rules.name: MD5 Used for Password Hashing
rules.note: MD5 has been broken since 2004 — billion-hash-per-second rainbow tables exist. Any MD5'd password is recoverable in minutes. AI uses it because old tutorials still teach it.
rules.remediation: Use bcrypt (recommended), argon2id, or scrypt with proper salt and cost factor. Never MD5 a password, even with salt.

rules.name: SHA-1 Used for Password Hashing
rules.note: SHA-1 is broken (Google's SHAttered, 2017) and is also a fast hash — wrong choice for passwords even when it wasn't broken. AI substitutes SHA-1 for MD5 thinking it's an upgrade.
rules.remediation: Use bcrypt, argon2id, or scrypt. Password hashing requires a slow, memory-hard, salted KDF — not a general-purpose hash.

rules.name: createCipheriv Called With Hardcoded String IV
rules.note: The IV passed to crypto.createCipheriv is a string literal. A constant IV reused across encryptions leaks plaintext patterns under any block cipher mode — for CBC, identical plaintexts produce identical ciphertexts; for GCM, IV reuse fully breaks confidentiality and authenticity.
rules.remediation: Generate a fresh IV per encryption: const iv = crypto.randomBytes(16). Prepend it to the ciphertext (it doesn't need to be secret, just unpredictable).

rules.name: AES-ECB Mode Used
rules.note: ECB mode encrypts each block independently — identical plaintext blocks produce identical ciphertext blocks. The 'ECB penguin' image demonstrates the leak visually. There is no good reason to use ECB in 2026.
rules.remediation: Use AES-GCM (preferred — authenticated encryption) or AES-CBC with a fresh random IV per message and a separate HMAC for integrity.

rules.name: crypto.createCipher() Deprecated API
rules.note: crypto.createCipher (no 'iv') was deprecated in Node 10 because it derives the key from a password via a weak KDF and uses no IV. It is functionally insecure regardless of the algorithm name passed.
rules.remediation: Switch to crypto.createCipheriv(algorithm, key, iv) with key from crypto.scrypt() or crypto.pbkdf2() and iv from crypto.randomBytes(16).

rules.name: RSA-PKCS1 v1.5 Padding (Use OAEP)
rules.note: RSA_PKCS1_PADDING is vulnerable to Bleichenbacher's attack — a network attacker can decrypt ciphertexts using only the server's responses to malformed messages. Variants of this attack have broken TLS, XML encryption, and JWT implementations for two decades.
rules.remediation: Use RSA_PKCS1_OAEP_PADDING for encryption and RSA_PKCS1_PSS_PADDING for signatures. If you control both sides, prefer hybrid encryption (RSA-OAEP wrapping an AES-GCM key) or switch to libsodium / age.

rules.name: Static Salt in PBKDF2 / scrypt
rules.note: PBKDF2 / scrypt is called with a string-literal salt. A static salt across all passwords means an attacker who breaks one hash gets a rainbow table for every user — the whole point of salting is defeated.
rules.remediation: Generate a unique random salt per password: const salt = crypto.randomBytes(16). Store the salt alongside the hash; you'll need it for verification.

rules.name: HMAC Digest Compared With == (Not timingSafeEqual)
rules.note: An HMAC digest is compared with == / === / !==. String equality returns false on the first mismatched byte, leaking timing information — a remote attacker can recover a valid signature one byte at a time. This is the same defect as FW78 but fires on any file, not just framework-detected projects.
rules.remediation: crypto.timingSafeEqual(Buffer.from(expected, 'hex'), Buffer.from(received, 'hex')). Both Buffers must be the same length — return early on length mismatch.

rules.name: Python hashlib.md5 / sha1 Used for Password Hashing
rules.note: hashlib.md5 / hashlib.sha1 used in a file that references 'password'. Both are broken for password storage — fast hashes plus an attack on the primitive itself. AI copies this pattern from old Python tutorials.
rules.remediation: Use passlib (argon2 / bcrypt) or hashlib.scrypt with a unique random salt. Never plain-hash a password regardless of the algorithm.
